Q11.2:
Firstly, as someone who has read a lot of T&C documents, I want to complement you on crafting a great example of what they're like to read. I particularly enjoyed the forced arbitration and ongoing consent clauses.

After reading the textual version, I don't think the visual T&C did a good job capturing the details as presented in the text. The icons for Machine Learning Accuracy, Fairness, and Potential Risk don't adequately describe the terms in the document, and I question their usefulness in general. For example, how could the owner of an LLM guarantee possibly guarantee the outputs to be accurate and unbiased? Who measures and attests to the validity of these claims. 

Further, where is the visual icon that informs the user that if Gaia violates the T&C, the user has implicitly agreed, by using the Gaia, to forced arbitration? It is also unclear what sections of the text "Machine Learning Potential Risk - Risky" is supposed to represent.
Q21.2:
In one of the introductory slides, you described the visual version of the T&C by saying "This version highlights key information of the Terms and Conditions that would present in a text-only version and organized into easy-to-follow categories". This implies a positive quality of the visual method, while implying the opposite for the textual version. 

Additionally, I think the format of this survey is flawed. I think the utility of the visual version of the T&C is in its ability to present information at a glance. If I had access to the visual guide while answering the survey questions, I would be able to answer both confidently and quickly. I am reminded of WHMIS labels and data sheets that both highlight important information with a visual symbol on the label and provide detailed textual descriptions in the MSDS.
Q23.8:
To summarize my earlier comments, I see the value in the visual format, but I don't think it is an adequate replacement for a textual T&C document. These are legal contracts and contain a lot of nuanced information. If there could be a way to attach some sort of "safety label" to a platform that uses visual symbols to remind the user about key details of the T&C, I think that could be incredibly useful. 

I also don't understand why some categories and icons were chosen. One example is the icons used for who data is sold to, i.e., Future Employers and Insurance Companies, when I think the textual version said data was available for general purchase. By enumerating only some of the possible purchasers, it seems to falsely limit the scope of the actual terms.

All this said, I am glad that someone is researching this topic. Thank you for your time and dedication.
